Patch Set 1:

What is the justification for the change from EINTR to ERESTARTSYS?  I thought the former is what should be returned to userspace when a process is interrupted before completing any IO, while (AFAIK, haven't verified) that ERESTARTSYS is caught in the kernel and actually restarts the syscall?

Patch Set 1:

Yes, mostly, but -ERESTARTSYS doesn't *force* a restart, it makes one possible if userspace has asked for it by setting the SA_RESTART flag on a sigaction.  If the flag is not set, ERESTARTSYS is translated to EINTR.

We've made this change elsewhere, in LU-3020 and LU-3581 - I wrote up a slightly long-winded explanation of it here:
https://jira.hpdd.intel.com/browse/LU-3020

Sorry to reference my own previous comments, it's surprisingly hard to find references explaining this behavior...

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(3 comments)

I'm now OK with the concept, but I think there is a latent problem in the patch that needs to be fixed, see defect below.

Patch Set 1:

Oof, thanks for finding that defect.

Broader focus:
I originally assumed that Lustre was using -EINTR for good reason.  It's quite rare in Linux filesystem code in general, only other uses I remember seeing were in NFS (In that specific instance, I think I concluded restartability was not an option).  Since it sounds like there's no particular reason, I'll see about making the larger change from -EINTR to -ERESTARTSYS for this patch.  Now's a good time to start a patch like that for 2.10.

parents.subject: LU-11102 test: test fewer files on ZFS system

commit.subject: LU-8494 llite: Return ERESTARTSYS for EINTR
commit.message:
LU-8494 llite: Return ERESTARTSYS for EINTR

So that it could retry syscall when the process is interrupted by
signal.

Clean up code in page fault handling.

Cray-bug-id: LUS-1372
Signed-off-by: Jinshan Xiong <jinshan.xiong@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Chris Horn <hornc@cray.com>
Change-Id: I96ab53d522adc61cd38b4d8f008ceabaaa0dc29b
